carrier_description: High-purity ammonia (typically ≥99.9%) used as a fuel in industrial and emerging energy applications. Requires specialized storage and handling due to its corrosive nature and volatility. Commonly produced via the Haber-Bosch process, though renewable variants exist.
Grid-level electricity transmitted at high voltage (380–220 kV), representing standard transmission infrastructure constraints and voltage level specifications.

Methane (CH4) is a hydrocarbon gas with typical purity levels for fuel use, commonly utilized in energy systems for combustion and as a feedstock in industrial processes. Infrastructure context includes pipeline transportation, storage, and combustion systems.

Grid-level electricity distributed at medium voltage (1–36 kV), typically used for residential and commercial supply. Standard purity and quality constraints align with European grid electricity specifications.

High-temperature heat used in industrial applications, typically requiring high thermal stability and compatibility with materials. Infrastructure context includes industrial heating systems and high-temperature process equipment.

Electricity used within buildings, typically generated on-site or from local distributed sources, not transmitted over the main grid. Purity and composition align with standard electrical grid specifications, but infrastructure is limited to building-level distribution.

Thermal energy used in buildings for space heating and domestic hot water. Typically provided through heating systems such as boilers, heat pumps, or district heating networks. Infrastructure context includes building heating systems and associated distribution networks. Purity and chemical composition constraints are generally not applicable as it refers to thermal energy rather than a chemical substance.

Geothermal heat from underground sources. Infrastructure requires drilling and heat exchangers. Typical temperature range: 10–30°C. No specific purity constraints. Used in district heating and industrial process heat applications.

Hydrogen (H2) typically requires high purity (≥99.9%) for most applications. It is stored and transported via pressurized tanks, pipelines, or liquid form. Infrastructure compatibility depends on purity and pressure standards.

Energy stored in the form of gravitational potential energy in water, requiring two reservoirs at different elevations. Water quality must be suitable to minimize sedimentation and wear on infrastructure. Infrastructure context includes geographical features such as mountains or valleys for elevation differences.
Renewable electricity generated from continuous water flow in run-of-river hydro systems, typically with minimal water storage infrastructure. Operates under standard grid compatibility constraints.

Hydro storage (reservoir) refers to the storage of energy in the form of potential energy in water within reservoirs. It typically involves infrastructure such as dams and turbines, with water quality considerations for system efficiency and longevity.

Heterogeneous mixture of household and commercial waste, primarily used for energy recovery through incineration or anaerobic digestion. Composition varies by region and includes organic, inorganic, and recyclable materials. Subject to local waste management regulations and quality standards for energy recovery.

Electricity generated from photovoltaic installations in alpine regions, accounting for environmental conditions such as high altitude, solar irradiance variability, and potential snow coverage impacts on panel efficiency.
Solar irradiation used for rooftop photovoltaic systems, with no specific purity constraints. Infrastructure context relates to solar panel installation and irradiance availability.

Nuclear fuel consisting of uranium-235 isotope, typically enriched to 3-5% concentration for use in pressurised water reactors. Requires stringent handling protocols due to radioactivity and criticality risks. Infrastructure context includes nuclear power plant fuel cycles and enrichment facilities.
Electricity generated from wind turbines, integrated into the grid infrastructure. No chemical purity constraints apply as it is a standard electricity carrier.

Wood fuel typically refers to dry, solid biomass with standard moisture content (≤20%) and calorific value (≈18–20 MJ/kg LHV). Commonly used in thermal systems for combustion-based heat generation, requiring preprocessing for size reduction and moisture control.

High-temperature process heat typically used in industrial applications, requiring specific temperature and pressure conditions. Infrastructure context involves industrial boilers, heat exchangers, and high-temperature piping systems.
Electricity used in non-residential buildings and facilities, typically distributed via low-voltage networks and consumed on-site without grid export.

Space cooling, typically delivered through HVAC systems and refrigeration technologies. Infrastructure context involves building-specific heating, ventilation, and air conditioning systems operating within standard temperature ranges for comfort and industrial processes.

Low-temperature heat typically used in space heating applications, domestic hot water, and low-temperature industrial processes. Commonly distributed via heating systems or district heating networks at temperatures below 90°C.

C₁₂ hydrocarbons representing diesel fuel, typically characterized by a boiling range of 180–360°C, with specifications for sulfur content (<10 ppm), viscosity, and cetane number. Infrastructure includes pipelines, storage tanks, and internal combustion engines optimized for liquid hydrocarbon fuels.

Diatomic nitrogen gas (N₂) with typical purity ranges of 99.5–99.999% depending on application. Commonly used as an inert gas in industrial processes, chemical synthesis, and as a feedstock for ammonia production. Infrastructure context includes storage as compressed gas or liquid nitrogen.

Wet biomass, typically high in moisture content and organic matter, often sourced from sewage sludge, food waste, or agricultural residues. May contain impurities and is processed in anaerobic digestion or combustion facilities for energy recovery. Infrastructure context includes waste treatment plants and biogas facilities.

CO2Sys refers to carbon dioxide utilized within the energy system, typically captured from industrial processes or the atmosphere. It is used in applications such as enhanced oil recovery, chemical synthesis, and carbon storage. Purity requirements vary by application, with industrial uses often requiring high-purity CO2 (99.9%+), while lower-purity streams may be used in other processes. Infrastructure context includes transportation via pipelines, storage in geological formations, and integration into industrial facilities.

Purified water (H2O) with standard purity constraints for use in energy systems, typically demineralized or deionized to prevent scaling and corrosion in equipment. Commonly used in cooling, steam generation, and as a reactant in chemical processes.

Typically requires low moisture content (<1%) and limited impurities (e.g., <0.5% free fatty acids). Composed primarily of triglycerides (C57H104O6) with variable chain lengths. Infrastructure requires compatible storage tanks and pumping systems for liquid handling.
Typically 99.9% pure for energy applications; compatible with existing fuel infrastructure but requires corrosion-resistant materials in pipelines and storage.

Carbon monoxide used as a resource within the energy system, typically in chemical processes or synthesis. Purity constraints may vary depending on specific applications, but generally requires high purity for use in fuel cells or as a feedstock in synthetic fuel production.

Carbon monoxide, typically produced as a byproduct of industrial processes or synthesis. Purity varies by application; commonly used in chemical synthesis and as a reducing agent. Infrastructure context includes pipeline transport in industrial settings.

Process hydrogen used in industrial applications, typically requiring high purity (e.g., 99.99%) and tailored for specific chemical processes. Infrastructure includes specialized pipelines and storage systems for industrial use.

Refined hydrocarbon blend typically meeting ASTM D439 standards for gasoline, with octane ratings between 87–93 RON. Used in internal combustion engines, distributed via pipelines, storage tanks, and tanker trucks. Infrastructure compatible with conventional fueling stations.

Oxygen (O2) is typically produced via air separation units (ASUs) and used in industrial processes such as steelmaking, chemical synthesis, and medical applications. Standard purity ranges from 99.5% to 99.999% depending on application. It is not a primary energy carrier but serves as a utility gas in various sectors.

Biochar produced from biomass pyrolysis, primarily composed of carbon. Purity varies based on feedstock and pyrolysis conditions. Typically handled as a solid material in storage and transportation infrastructure.

CO₂ captured from flue gas streams, typically containing impurities such as nitrogen, oxygen, and trace contaminants. Infrastructure context includes post-combustion capture systems at industrial point sources.

CO2 typically requires high purity (≥99.9%) for industrial applications, with chemical composition constrained to exclude contaminants like SOx, NOx, and particulates. Infrastructure context includes capture from flue gases, direct air capture, and geological storage.
